summary: Asparouhova continues exploring jhanas—altered states of consciousness accessible through concentration—after attending meditation retreats that profoundly changed her perspective. She wrote pragmatic, non-spiritual instructions for reaching jhana states that proved successful for newcomers. Partnering with the retreat company Jhourney, she investigated whether meditation experience correlates with jhana success and found no correlation. This finding leads her to question whether jhanas should even be classified as a 'meditation practice' and whether meditation itself is being confused with the deeper skills it aims to develop.
key_points: Asparouhova progressed through all eight or nine jhanic states in roughly 20 cumulative hours, far faster than the months or years traditionally expected
An analysis of anonymized meditator data found no correlation between meditation experience and success with jhanas, challenging conventional assumptions
The finding raises fundamental questions about what meditation experience actually measures and whether meditation is a means or an end
She hypothesizes that spending her days in focused creative states through writing and research may have provided the underlying attentional skills that meditation typically develops
money_quotes: I went back to try it again, and accidentally discovered how to voluntarily turn my consciousness on and off, which occasioned an experience so profound that I decided not to write about it.
We did not find any correlation between meditation experience and success with the jhanas.
This finding casts into question why jhanas are described as a 'meditation practice' at all.
I think meditation is a finger, and we don't know what the moon is yet.
Someone who meditates every day is not necessarily adept at that skill. They're just someone who meditates often.
We shouldn't conflate 'meditation experience' with the underlying skills it develops.
Even though I don't formally meditate, I am very lucky to spend pretty much all day in a focused, creative state, thanks to the nature of my work.
key_insight: Meditation may be merely one method—a finger pointing at the moon—for developing underlying attentional skills, and we should stop conflating the practice itself with the deeper capacities it aims to cultivate.
